Overall, the paper may contain a promising intuition, but in its current form the method is under-specified, the theoretical rationale is weak, and the writing and positioning need substantial revision before the work can be properly evaluated.

comments.title: Classifier outputs/softmax probabilities are repeatedly treated as manifold distances without justification
comments.quote: In this paper, we propose a manifold distance detection method ... by measuring the different distances on the manifold. / Since the probability output of the deep learning model can reflect the distance of manifold distance between different samples ... / The size of the probability output represents the shape of the input x in the low-dimensional manifold mapping and the distance from this type of feature...
comments.explanation: The paper's central rationale for MDJ appears to identify changes in softmax/model probabilities after preprocessing with distances on, or from, a data manifold. This is not generally valid without further assumptions. Softmax values are normalized logits and can be altered by calibration, temperature, logit scaling, or architecture choices without corresponding changes in geometric distance. Neural networks can also assign high confidence to off-manifold or adversarial inputs. The text also sometimes describes ordinary DNN classifiers as explicitly mapping inputs to low-dimensional manifolds and measuring distance from stored decision boundaries, which is not how standard ImageNet classifiers operate unless a specific embedding, manifold estimator, and distance rule are defined. Because this proxy is central to the proposed detector, the paper needs a precise definition of the claimed distance, a derivation or calibration argument connecting it to model outputs, or more cautious terminology such as softmax-confidence divergence.

comments.title: White-box and preprocessing-defense claims lack an adaptive-attacker threat model
comments.quote: Tests on the ImageNet dataset, the MDJ is effective against the most adversarial samples under whitebox, graybox, and blackbox attack scenarios. / Processing-based Techniques: The defense method based on image preprocessing has a defensive effect on migration attacks and gradient attacks. / For legitimate samples, this transformation does not change the mapping position much. But for adversarial samples, different transformations change the spatial mapping of the picture a lot...
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly claims that MDJ or preprocessing transformations are effective against white-box, gradient, transfer, and black-box attacks, but it does not clearly specify whether the attacker is adaptive to the full defense. For preprocessing-based detectors, a meaningful white-box evaluation should account for the preprocessing distribution, detector threshold, and decision rule, often using BPDA/EOT or adaptive objectives when transformations are random or non-differentiable. The claim that preprocessing moves adversarial samples much more than legitimate samples may hold for some non-adaptive attacks, but it is not generally true: benign images can be affected by aggressive transformations, and adaptive adversarial examples can be optimized through transformations. Without this threat-model clarification, the robustness claims risk overstating what the experiments establish.

comments.title: Image adversarial attacks are described using graph-attack terminology
comments.quote: Attackers can deceive the DNNs model by manipulating graph structure or node features, which results in graph counter disturbances, and limits their application in safety-critical systems.
comments.explanation: The paper's stated setting and experiments concern image classification on ImageNet, but this sentence uses terminology from graph neural network attacks: graph structure, node features, and graph perturbations. If the authors intend only image perturbations, the sentence should be revised to avoid confusing the data domain and threat model. If they intend to cover graph attacks as well, the method and experiments do not appear to support that broader scope.

comments.title: Attack taxonomy conflates transfer attacks with query-based black-box attacks
comments.quote: In the transfer attack model, because the attacker does not know the internal information of the model, it is necessary to query the target model multiple times, use the alternative model and other optimization methods to solve the parameters based on the query results...
comments.explanation: The description makes repeated target-model queries necessary for transfer attacks. In the standard adversarial-example literature, transfer attacks and query-based black-box attacks are distinct, though related. A transfer attack may use a surrogate model trained independently or on public/similar data and then transfer adversarial examples to the target with few or no target queries. Query-based substitute-model training is one possible black-box strategy, not a defining property of transferability. This matters because later gray-box and black-box evaluation claims depend on these categories.

comments.title: Accuracy, generalization, overfitting, and adversarial inevitability claims are overbroad
comments.quote: we can prove that in the deep learning model, the accuracy and generalization of the model cannot be taken into account at the same time. / too few data points cause the boundary shape to change during smoothing, which makes the appearance of adversarial samples inevitable...
comments.explanation: The paper appears to allude to real themes in robustness theory, such as tradeoffs among standard accuracy, robust accuracy, sample complexity, boundary complexity, and generalization. However, the statements as written are much broader than standard results support. It is not generally true that accuracy and generalization cannot both be improved, nor is adversarial vulnerability made inevitable merely by boundary smoothing or by pursuing boundary accuracy. Such claims depend on the data distribution, threat model, classifier class, training objective, and robustness definition. The authors should either provide formal assumptions and proof or narrow the claims to established robustness/generalization tradeoffs.

comments.title: “We prove” overstates what appears to be an empirical comparison of defense combinations
comments.quote: Moreover, we prove that there is an orchestration strategy which is a simple defense combination method in the image field, and its effect is better than the single simple defense, and the random combination of the weak defense combination method.
comments.explanation: The contribution appears to be an empirical finding that a selected combination of preprocessing and detection steps can outperform individual or random combinations on the evaluated ImageNet attacks. But the wording 'we prove' suggests a formal guarantee that the orchestration strategy is better than single or random weak defenses. No theorem, assumptions, or formal objective are stated. The claim should be supported by a proof or rephrased as an empirical result.

comments.title: Adversarial training is mischaracterized as detection or as defending only against attacks seen during training
comments.quote: Adversarial Training: The defense method is to superimpose the disturbance that maximizes the loss function in the sample... even if the attack method with obvious changes in the model gradient is easy to be detected. / this method can only defend against attacks added in the training phase...
comments.explanation: The paper correctly gestures at training with high-loss or worst-case perturbations, but then misframes adversarial training as detecting attacks with obvious gradient changes and as defending only against the exact attacks included during training. Standard adversarial training is usually formulated as robust optimization over a specified perturbation set, not as a test-time detector or mere memorization of attack samples. It is fair to say that adversarial training often generalizes best within the threat model used during training and may fail under different or stronger threat models, but the paper's current wording is too strong and technically misleading.

comments.title: Binary-classification argument does not clearly distinguish adversarial examples from legitimate negatives
comments.quote: From the perspective of binary classification, there is no difference between adversarial samples and correctly classified negative samples.
comments.explanation: The intended point may be that an adversarial example can lie on the wrong side of a binary decision boundary and therefore resemble a negative example from the perspective of the original class. But adversarial examples are misclassified relative to their true label, whereas correctly classified negative samples are not. The claim needs to specify whether 'negative' is with respect to the original class, the predicted class, or a one-vs-rest setup. Without that precision, the statement undercuts the paper's own motivation that adversarial and legitimate samples occupy detectably different manifold positions.

comments.title: The proposed detection proposition is not a valid consequence of the manifold assumption
comments.quote: We can assume that the manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M and algorithm A, if x is distributed as P(X), then x is on M. The defense detection uses its negative proposition B: that is, there are manifold M* and algorithm A*. If the distribution of x is not P(X), then x is not on M*.
comments.explanation: Proposition A roughly states that if x is drawn from P(X), then x lies on M. The proposed detection proposition B states that if x is not drawn from P(X), then x is not on M*. That is not the logical negation or contrapositive of A; it is an unsupported inverse/converse-like claim. The contrapositive would be: if x is not on M, then x is not drawn from P(X). Moreover, different distributions can share support or overlap on the same manifold, and adversarial examples may lie near or on a data manifold depending on the attack and semantic setting. Thus the proposed detection basis does not follow from the manifold hypothesis alone.

comments.title: Increasing manifold dimensionality is asserted as a solution without definition or support
comments.quote: The classification model is easy to be attacked because the dimensionality of the manifold has been excessively reduced. Increasing the dimensionality of the manifold can distinguish adversarial samples, but it is difficult to solve the direction of increasing dimensionality.
comments.explanation: The paper suggests that adversarial vulnerability is caused by excessive reduction of manifold dimensionality and that increasing dimensionality can separate adversarial samples. This is not established. The text does not define which manifold's dimensionality is reduced, how the classifier reduces it, what operation would increase it, or why increasing it would distinguish adversarial examples rather than add irrelevant variation. Adversarial vulnerability can arise from high-dimensional geometry, non-robust features, decision-boundary proximity, optimization effects, and distributional assumptions; it is not generally reducible to excessive manifold compression.

comments.title: Attack objective is oversimplified as fastest gradient descent
comments.quote: From the perspective of manifold analysis, the purpose of the attack method is to find the fastest direction of gradient descent, derive the gradient, so that the shape of the adversarial sample in the high-dimensional space changes.
comments.explanation: This description is technically misleading. Untargeted attacks such as FGSM are commonly described as increasing the loss with respect to the true label, i.e. gradient ascent on the loss, while targeted attacks often minimize loss toward a chosen target class. Many attacks solve constrained optimization problems rather than simply following a steepest direction, and transfer attacks may use gradients of a substitute model. Since the paper discusses multiple attack families, the 'fastest gradient descent' formulation does not accurately describe the full set of attacks.

comments.title: MDJ, Comb1–Comb3, thresholds, and randomized transformations are underspecified for reproduction
comments.quote: We use a simple method, to reflect the transformation of the distance of the picture manifold. / Comb1 ... Comb2 ... Comb3 ... / the ability to reach a detection model above 0.65 thresholds is weak. / Comb represent the Com3 in the previous Section4.
comments.explanation: The core detector and orchestration procedures are not specified in enough detail to reproduce the reported results. The paper does not clearly define the statistic used for 'manifold distance' detection: maximum softmax probability, original-class probability, margin, KL divergence, probability drop, rank change, or another quantity. It also does not clearly define threshold direction, threshold selection, whether thresholds are attack-specific or global, or the false-positive operating point used for calibration. For Comb1–Comb3, the logic combining label changes and threshold tests is ambiguous, including whether conditions are joined by OR or AND and whether samples above a threshold are declared benign or adversarial. Randomized preprocessing details are also incomplete, such as whether pixel crop ranges are integer-valued, how cropped images are resized, how many random draws are used, and how stochastic outputs are aggregated. Because these choices directly determine the reported detection rates, this is a major reproducibility problem.

comments.title: Defensive distillation discussion is incomplete and overstates the role of a perfect boundary
comments.quote: defensive distillation: whether it can build its optimized network is critical to the effectiveness of this defense method. This is determined by whether the optimized network can accurately obtain the optimal discrimination boundary. If the learning effect of the boundary is perfect, the performance of defense against known attacks will be greatly improved. But for unknown attacks, the sample
comments.explanation: The paragraph appears to stop mid-thought, leaving the caveat about unknown attacks incomplete. More substantively, the claim that a perfectly learned discrimination boundary would greatly improve defense against known attacks is not justified. Defensive distillation was later shown in many settings to rely on gradient masking rather than genuinely robust boundaries, and an accurate clean-data boundary can still be adversarially vulnerable. The paper should clarify what 'optimal discrimination boundary' means and why it would imply adversarial robustness.

comments.title: Formal attack-success condition only covers untargeted attacks
comments.quote: given a normal example whose logit is L, the goal of the attacker is to perturb the example to get a new logit L2 such that rank(L,1) ≠ rank(L2,1)
comments.explanation: This condition expresses untargeted misclassification: the top-ranked class changes after perturbation. That is fine if the paper is explicitly defining untargeted attacks on initially correctly classified samples. However, the paper also discusses attacks such as CW and JSMA, which are often evaluated in targeted forms. For targeted attacks, the success condition is usually that the specified target class becomes top-ranked, not merely that the original top class changes. The paper should also state whether the original top-ranked class is assumed to equal the true label.

comments.title: CW attack explanations conflict with CW definitions and with the reported table interpretation
comments.quote: in a CW attack, the attack only changes a limited number of pixels. / the Next class as the targeted attack has a greater impact on the detection model, resulting in an increase in the detection rate of the least-likely class as the targeted attack.
comments.explanation: The statement that CW attacks change only a limited number of pixels applies at most to an L0-constrained CW attack. CW L2 and L∞ attacks generally optimize different norms and may alter many or all pixels by small amounts, so a pixel-sparsity explanation cannot be applied to CW attacks generally. The discussion of least-likely versus next-class targets is also internally confusing: it says the Next-class attack has a greater impact but then attributes the increased detection rate to least-likely targets. The reported table appears to show higher detection for least-likely than Next in several CW rows. The explanation should be aligned with the specific CW variants and the actual table values.

comments.title: Evaluation relies on recall/detection rates without matched false-positive rates or sufficient operating-point details
comments.quote: Table 1 and Table 2 shows the detection rates for successful adversarial examples for each attack method. / We compare our results with Feature Squeezing ... It shows that our recall value ... is significantly higher... / improve the accuracy of the model.
comments.explanation: The reported tables focus on adversarial detection rates or recall, but detector performance cannot be assessed without corresponding benign false-positive rates, ROC/AUC, precision, clean accuracy, or recall at a fixed false-positive rate. A detector can obtain high recall simply by rejecting many benign inputs. This also affects the comparison with Feature Squeezing: a fair comparison should use matched operating points or a shared validation/calibration protocol, not recall alone. The paper further interprets higher detection rates as improved model accuracy or classification efficiency, but MDJ appears to be an external detector rather than a modification of the classifier; system-level accuracy depends on both adversarial rejection and benign false positives. Claims of 'significant' improvement and coverage of 'all attacks' should also be limited to the evaluated attacks and supported by variance estimates or statistical tests if statistical significance is intended.

comments.title: Prose claim that all CW samples are detected conflicts with Table 5 and uses inconsistent metrics
comments.quote: especially on CW attacks, we can detect all pairs of samples, and the best classification rate of feature squeezing is only 0.87.
comments.explanation: Several CW entries for the Comb method appear to be 1.00, but at least one CW row is reported as 0.99 rather than 1.00. Thus the statement that the method detects all CW samples is not consistent with the table as printed. The same sentence also calls Feature Squeezing's value a 'classification rate' while the table labels the metric as Recall, which can mislead readers about what is being compared.

comments.title: Citation appears to support poisoning attacks rather than the evasion attacks discussed in context
comments.quote: Luis Muñoz-González, Battista Biggio, Ambra Demontis, Andrea Paudice, Vasin Wongrassamee, Emil C Lupu, and Fabio Roli. Towards poisoning of deep learning algorithms with back-gradient optimization...
comments.explanation: The cited work is about training-time poisoning via back-gradient optimization. The surrounding context discusses white-box gradient optimization attacks such as FGSM, BIM, DeepFool, JSMA, and CW, which are primarily test-time evasion attacks. If the citation is intended to support the evasion-attack formulation, it is mismatched. The authors should cite standard evasion/adversarial-example references for that claim or clarify that the cited paper is only an example of gradient-based adversarial optimization more broadly.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important problem—detecting adversarial examples without modifying the protected classifier—and the idea of combining manifold-distance-based detection with preprocessing “orchestration” could be interesting if made precise and rigorously evaluated. However, the current presentation is substantially below the clarity and technical standard expected for ICLR. The motivation relies heavily on broad and sometimes inaccurate claims about the manifold hypothesis, accuracy–generalization tradeoffs, and the inevitability of adversarial examples, but these claims are not formally justified or clearly connected to the proposed method. Key concepts such as “manifold distance,” how it is computed, what manifold is estimated, how preprocessing methods are selected, and what “service orchestration” means are not defined in the provided text, making the contribution difficult to assess. The related-work discussion is shallow and contains factual/terminological issues, grammatical problems, and outdated framing; for example, adversarial training, defensive distillation, and preprocessing are described only at a high level, without engaging with known failures of many detection/preprocessing defenses under adaptive attacks. The writing quality is a major concern: there are frequent language errors, awkward phrasing, missing spaces/citations, inconsistent terminology, and statements that appear unrelated or confused, such as references to graph attacks in an image-classification context. The experimental claims in the abstract are potentially significant, especially results on ImageNet and CW/DI-FGSM, but they will require much stronger support: carefully defined threat models, adaptive white-box attacks against the full detector pipeline, comparisons to stronger modern baselines, false-positive/false-negative tradeoffs, clean-accuracy impact, computational cost, and ablations showing why orchestration improves over individual transformations. Overall, the paper may contain a promising intuition, but in its current form the method is under-specified, the theoretical rationale is weak, and the writing and positioning need substantial revision before the work can be properly evaluated.

comments.title: Softmax/probability-based “manifold distance” is asserted without a clear mathematical basis
comments.quote: In this paper, we propose a manifold distance detection method to distinguish between legitimate samples and adversarial samples by measuring the different distances on the manifold.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as an abstract-level shorthand and looked to the surrounding context for a later formal definition of the claimed distance. The later method description indicates that the detector uses model output probabilities/softmax changes after preprocessing as the operative signal. That helps explain what the authors intend operationally, but it does not by itself justify calling the quantity a distance “on the manifold.” In standard usage, softmax confidence or probability divergence is not a metric on the data manifold, nor is it reliably proportional to distance from the data manifold or decision boundary without additional calibration or assumptions. This matters because the central conceptual claim of MDJ is that preprocessing expands a manifold distance between benign and adversarial samples. The paper needs either a precise definition of this distance and its relation to the manifold hypothesis, or more cautious terminology such as confidence/probability divergence.

comments.title: White-box robustness claim is ambiguous without an adaptive-attacker threat model
comments.quote: Tests on the ImageNet dataset, the MDJ is effective against the most adversarial samples under whitebox, graybox, and blackbox attack scenarios.
comments.explanation: The abstract is making a central robustness claim, so I checked whether the surrounding text clarifies what “whitebox” means. The related-work section lists standard attacks such as FGSM, BIM, DeepFool, JSMA, CW, MI-FGSM, and DI-FGSM, and the method later relies on randomized preprocessing plus a detector. However, in adversarial-defense evaluation, a white-box claim usually means the attacker knows and differentiates/optimizes through the complete defense, including the preprocessing distribution and detection rule, often using adaptive objectives or BPDA/EOT when preprocessing is non-differentiable or randomized. The passage does not distinguish this from attacks generated only against the underlying classifier. Since preprocessing-based defenses have often appeared effective against non-adaptive attacks but failed under adaptive evaluation, the claim as written may substantially overstate what has been shown unless the experiments include such adaptive white-box attacks.

comments.title: Image adversarial attacks are described using graph-attack terminology
comments.quote: Attackers can deceive the DNNs model by manipulating graph structure or node features, which results in graph counter disturbances, and limits their application in safety-critical systems.
comments.explanation: I tried to interpret this generously as a broad statement about adversarial attacks across domains, but the paper’s stated setting and experiments are image classification on ImageNet, and the preceding abstract describes adversarial examples as specially crafted perturbations to images. “Manipulating graph structure or node features” is terminology from graph neural network attacks, not standard image attacks. This is not just wording: it could confuse the reader about the threat model and data domain being defended. If the authors intend only image perturbations, this sentence should be revised; if they intend graph attacks as well, the later method and experiments do not appear to support that broader scope.

comments.title: Attack taxonomy conflates transfer attacks with query-based black-box attacks
comments.quote: In the transfer attack model, because the attacker does not know the internal information of the model, it is necessary to query the target model multiple times, use the alternative model and other optimization methods to solve the parameters based on the query results, generate the adversarial sample according to the gradient of the alternative model, and classify model attacks.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to contrast white-box gradient attacks with attacks using an alternative model, which is a useful distinction. However, the description makes repeated target-model queries “necessary” for transfer attacks. In the standard adversarial-example literature, transfer attacks and query-based black-box attacks are related but distinct: a transfer attack may use a surrogate model trained independently, or on public/similar data, and then transfer examples to the target with few or no target queries. Query-based substitute-model training is one possible black-box strategy, not a defining requirement of transferability. This matters because the later claims about gray-box/black-box effectiveness depend on threat-model categories; imprecise taxonomy can make the evaluation appear broader than it is.

comments.title: Claim that accuracy and generalization cannot both be achieved is overbroad
comments.quote: In the analysis of defense methods using the manifold hypothesis, we can prove that in the deep learning model, the accuracy and generalization of the model cannot be taken into account at the same time. That is, when we improve the performance of one aspect, we will sacrifice another one.
comments.explanation: I read this as possibly alluding to known tradeoffs between standard accuracy and adversarial robustness, or between boundary complexity and generalization. But the statement as written is much broader: it says accuracy and generalization in deep learning cannot be taken into account at the same time. That is not a standard theorem, and many learning objectives explicitly aim to improve both empirical accuracy and generalization. The surrounding context does not provide a formal proof or specify restrictive assumptions under which such a tradeoff would hold. If the intended point is the standard robustness–accuracy tradeoff or an overfitting tradeoff, the claim should be narrowed and stated with appropriate conditions.

comments.title: “We prove” is inconsistent with an apparently empirical comparison of defense combinations
comments.quote: Moreover, we prove that there is an orchestration strategy which is a simple defense combination method in the image field, and its effect is better than the single simple defense, and the random combination of the weak defense combination method.
comments.explanation: The contribution is understandable: the authors want to argue that a selected/orchestrated combination of preprocessing defenses can outperform individual or random combinations. I checked whether this might be a forward reference to a formal theorem, but the surrounding full-paper context frames the result mainly through experiments on ImageNet and heuristic selection of preprocessing methods. The abstract itself says “We show empirically,” which is more appropriate. As written, “we prove” suggests a formal guarantee that the orchestration strategy is better than single or random weak defenses, but no assumptions, objective, or theorem are stated here. This should either be supported by a formal result or rephrased as an empirical finding.

comments.title: Adversarial training is described as a detection method rather than a robustness-training objective
comments.quote: _**Adversarial Training:**_ The defense method is to superimpose the disturbance that maximizes the loss function in the sample, so it is effective for fixed or large disturbances, even if the attack method with obvious changes in the model gradient is easy to be detected.
comments.explanation: I read this as an attempt to summarize adversarial training in the standard Goodfellow/Szegedy sense: augmenting training with worst-case or high-loss perturbations. That part is directionally right. However, the sentence then says attacks with obvious gradient changes are “easy to be detected,” which shifts from robustness training to detection. Standard adversarial training does not normally detect attacks by looking for gradient changes at test time; it changes the classifier parameters by solving an approximate robust optimization problem over a threat model. Also, its effectiveness is not simply for “fixed or large disturbances”: it is typically tied to a specified perturbation norm and radius, and larger perturbations are generally harder, not automatically easier. This is in a related-work section, so the issue is localized, but it risks misframing the baseline defense category used to motivate MDJ.

comments.title: Preprocessing defenses are stated as generally effective without adaptive-attack qualifications
comments.quote: _**Processing-based Techniques:**_ The defense method based on image preprocessing has a defensive effect on migration attacks and gradient attacks. Its essence is to reduce the noise of the sample, to reduce the interference of the adversarial disturbance to the model.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended point seems to be that input transformations such as filtering, compression, or resizing can sometimes reduce the effect of non-adaptive perturbations, and this is a reasonable motivation for including preprocessing in an orchestrated detector. What remains problematic is the unqualified claim that preprocessing has a defensive effect on transfer and gradient attacks. A large body of work, especially adaptive attacks using BPDA/EOT, shows that many preprocessing defenses provide little robustness once the attacker accounts for the transformation. The full paper later even mentions that some preprocessing defenses were defeated, so the context partially softens the claim, but this sentence as written could mislead readers into thinking preprocessing is generally robust rather than conditionally useful under particular attack models.

comments.title: Manifold hypothesis is conflated with the actual computation performed by a standard classifier
comments.quote: In the model checking stage, the features of the picture are also mapped on low dimensions, and the distance from the manifold determined in the learning process is measured to determine the label output by the model. In the detection stage, the model extracts the characteristics of the input picture and compares it with the stored optimal decision border to determine the classification of the input picture.
comments.explanation: I understand this as an intuitive manifold-based interpretation of classification: a trained network may learn representations in which samples from the same class cluster near lower-dimensional structures. As intuition, that is acceptable. But the text states it as if ordinary DNN classifiers explicitly map inputs to low-dimensional manifolds, measure distance from a learned manifold, and compare to a stored optimal decision border. That is not generally how standard ImageNet classifiers operate unless the paper defines a specific embedding, manifold estimator, and distance rule. Later context says MDJ will use softmax outputs as a proxy for manifold distance, but that does not resolve the stronger claim here that the protected model itself measures manifold distance. This matters because the proposed detector’s rationale depends on treating classifier probabilities as distances to a manifold/decision boundary.

comments.title: Claim of inevitable adversarial samples from boundary smoothing/overfitting is not justified
comments.quote: The reason is that too few data points cause the boundary shape to change during smoothing, which makes the appearance of adversarial samples inevitable; on the other hand, due to the excessive pursuit of the accuracy of the boundary, it leads to overfitting, and reduce the generalization and robustness of the model.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to connect sample sparsity, decision-boundary estimation, overfitting, and adversarial vulnerability, which are all relevant themes in robustness theory. However, the specific conclusion that boundary smoothing due to too few data points makes adversarial examples “inevitable” is asserted rather than derived. There are known tradeoffs between standard accuracy, robust accuracy, sample complexity, and distributional assumptions, but inevitability depends on the threat model, data distribution, classifier class, and robustness definition. The second half also seems to imply a direct monotonic tradeoff where pursuing boundary accuracy necessarily reduces generalization and robustness. That may be true in some regimes, but it is not a general theorem as stated. Because this claim supports the need for a separate detection system, the missing qualification is more than stylistic.

comments.title: Binary-classification argument does not clearly distinguish adversarial examples from legitimate negatives
comments.quote: From the perspective of binary classification, there is no difference between adversarial samples and correctly classified negative samples.
comments.explanation: I tried to interpret this charitably as saying that, from the target class’s perspective, an adversarial example may be placed on the wrong side of a binary decision boundary and therefore resembles a negative example. But the sentence says “correctly classified negative samples,” whereas adversarial examples are, by definition, misclassified relative to their true label. If the authors mean negatives with respect to the original class, that needs to be stated; if they mean negatives with respect to the predicted class, the comparison changes. The following discussion about legitimate samples, legitimate negative samples, and manifolds does not fully resolve the ambiguity. This matters because the detector is motivated by the idea that adversarial and legitimate samples have different manifold positions; saying there is “no difference” between adversarial and correctly classified negative samples cuts against that motivation unless the binary setup is made precise.

comments.title: Softmax/model decision probabilities are assumed to reveal adversarial-vs-legitimate distribution differences
comments.quote: The distribution probability of the adversarial sample and the legitimate sample is different, that is, the model decision probability of the adversarial sample and the legitimate sample should be different.
comments.explanation: The authors appear to be setting up the later MDJ idea that changes in softmax probability under preprocessing can be used as a detection signal. It is reasonable to test this empirically. The problem is the implication “distribution probability is different, that is, model decision probability should be different.” A classifier’s softmax output is not a reliable estimate of data density or distance to the data manifold, and adversarial examples are often optimized to be assigned high confidence by the model. Standard results on calibration and out-of-distribution behavior show that neural networks can be confidently wrong far from the training distribution. Thus, the statement needs either a formal assumption, a calibrated density/model-distance argument, or empirical qualification. As written, it overstates a key premise of the proposed detection method.

comments.title: The proposed negative proposition is not a valid converse of the manifold assumption
comments.quote: We can assume that the manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M and algorithm A, if x is distributed as P(X), then x is on M. The defense detection uses its negative proposition B: that is, there are manifold M* and algorithm A*. If the distribution of x is not P(X), then x is not on M*.
comments.explanation: This is the most formal-looking part of the passage, so I checked whether the logic follows from the stated manifold assumption. Proposition A says roughly: if x is drawn from P(X), then x lies on M. The stated detection proposition B says: if x is not drawn from P(X), then x is not on M*. That is not the logical negation or contrapositive of A; it is closer to an unsupported converse/inverse-style claim. The contrapositive of A would be: if x is not on M, then x is not distributed as P(X). Moreover, many distributions can share support or overlap on the same manifold, and an adversarial example can lie near or even on a data manifold depending on the attack and semantic setup. Therefore the claimed basis for detecting adversarial samples does not follow from the manifold hypothesis alone. This directly affects the theoretical justification for the proposed detector.

comments.title: Increasing manifold dimensionality is asserted as a route to distinguishing adversarial samples
comments.quote: The classification model is easy to be attacked because the dimensionality of the manifold has been excessively reduced. Increasing the dimensionality of the manifold can distinguish adversarial samples, but it is difficult to solve the direction of increasing dimensionality.
comments.explanation: I take the authors’ intent to be that overly compressed representations may discard useful information and that richer representations could help separate adversarial from benign inputs. That is a plausible intuition. But the statement as written makes a much stronger technical claim: that adversarial vulnerability is caused by excessive reduction of manifold dimensionality, and that increasing manifold dimensionality can distinguish adversarial samples. The passage does not define the manifold dimension being reduced, how a classifier reduces it, what operation would increase it, or why doing so would separate adversarial examples rather than also increasing noise or irrelevant variation. In robustness theory, vulnerability can arise from high-dimensional geometry, non-robust features, decision-boundary proximity, optimization, and distributional factors; it is not generally reducible to excessive manifold dimensionality reduction. Since this claim motivates the later orchestration strategy, it needs more justification or should be reframed as a hypothesis.

comments.title: Softmax probabilities are treated as manifold or boundary distances without justification
comments.quote: Since the probability output of the deep learning model can reflect the distance of manifold distance between different samples, combined with the above analysis of the preprocessing method that has the characteristics of defending against gradient optimization models and transfer attack models, it becomes an ideal orchestration strategy idea.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intended bridge: if preprocessing moves adversarial and benign inputs differently, then changes in the classifier’s output distribution might be used as a proxy for how far the input has moved relative to the learned class manifold or decision boundary. The later subsection reinforces this by saying that softmax probability is used for the manifold distance detector. However, the stated relationship is not generally valid without additional assumptions. Softmax probabilities are not geometric distances to a data manifold or even calibrated distances to a decision boundary; they can be changed by rescaling logits, temperature, calibration, or architecture choices while leaving the decision boundary unchanged. Standard observations about neural networks also show that softmax confidence can be high far off the data manifold. A margin in logit space may sometimes correlate with distance to a linearized boundary, but that is a different and more limited claim. Because MDJ appears to rely centrally on interpreting probability divergence as manifold distance, this needs either a derivation, an empirical calibration argument, or a more cautious framing.

comments.title: Preprocessing is assumed to move adversarial samples much more than legitimate samples
comments.quote: For legitimate samples, this transformation does not change the mapping position much. But for adversarial samples, different transformations change the spatial mapping of the picture a lot, which is verified in the subsequent experimental process.
comments.explanation: The intuition is reasonable for some non-adaptive adversarial examples: random crops, filtering, compression, or geometric distortions can disrupt perturbations more than semantic image content. I also see that the authors intend to validate this experimentally later. The concern is that the statement is phrased as a general property of legitimate versus adversarial samples, but it is not generally true. Legitimate images can be substantially affected by aggressive crops or swirls, especially for small or off-center objects, while adaptive adversarial examples can be optimized through randomized transformations using expectation over transformation techniques. This is especially important because the passage itself cites defenses that were defeated by Athalye et al. Without specifying that the claim is limited to the evaluated non-adaptive or partially adaptive attacks, the argument overstates what preprocessing can guarantee and risks making the detector look more principled than the evidence supports.

comments.title: Adversarial training is described as defending only against attacks included during training
comments.quote: _**Adversarial training:**_ this method can only defend against attacks added in the training phase, the reason is the model extracts the features of the adversarial samples formed by the attack method during training and thus can perform defense.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ practical point: adversarial training is often strongest against the threat model and attack procedure used during training, and it may not transfer well to qualitatively different perturbation sets or newly discovered attacks. That is a fair limitation. But the word “only” is too strong and technically inaccurate. Modern adversarial training is usually formulated as robust optimization over a perturbation set, not merely memorization or feature extraction from a fixed attack algorithm’s samples. A model trained with PGD-style adversarial training, for example, can defend against many attacks within the same norm ball even if those exact attack instances or algorithms were not used during training. The passage should distinguish between generalization within a specified threat model and failure under different or stronger threat models.

comments.title: Attack objective is oversimplified as fastest gradient descent
comments.quote: From the perspective of manifold analysis, the purpose of the attack method is to find the fastest direction of gradient descent, derive the gradient, so that the shape of the adversarial sample in the high-dimensional space changes.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to give an intuitive manifold-level explanation of gradient-based attacks, and for an overview section some simplification is acceptable. Still, the specific description is technically misleading. Untargeted attacks such as FGSM are commonly described as moving in the direction that increases the loss with respect to the true label, i.e. gradient ascent on the loss, while targeted attacks often minimize loss toward a target class. Some attacks also solve constrained optimization problems rather than simply following the steepest direction, and transfer attacks may use gradients of a substitute model rather than the protected model. The surrounding text says both optimization-model and migration/transfer-model attacks are covered, so the “fastest direction of gradient descent” formulation does not accurately describe the full set of attacks being discussed.

comments.title: The orchestration procedure is not specified enough to reproduce
comments.quote: Here, we propose the orchestration strategy Manifold Distance Judge (MDJ) and it uses the output of deep learning and preprocessing methods to expand the distance between manifolds. The MDJ was expressed in the form of combination methods in the experimentand achieved excellent results in the ImageNet dataset(Deng et al., 2009).
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is clear: combine preprocessing transformations with a detector based on changes in model outputs. However, the actual orchestration remains underspecified in this passage. A reader cannot tell how MDJ chooses the “best” preprocessing method, whether transformations are applied once or multiple times, how stochastic outputs are aggregated, what divergence or distance is computed, how thresholds are calibrated, or how false-positive/false-negative tradeoffs are selected. Some of these details may be introduced later, but this passage presents MDJ as the proposed method rather than merely a preview. Since the claimed contribution is an orchestration strategy rather than just a list of preprocessing defenses, these operational choices are central to reproducibility and to evaluating whether the gain comes from principled orchestration or from post hoc tuning.

comments.title: Preprocessing transformations leave important implementation details implicit
comments.quote: _**Random Zoom:**_ (Guo et al., 2017) considered cropping and rescaling of an image as one of their defenses, which is effectively zooming in on a portion of the image, an approach that was defeated by (Athalye et al., 2018b). We reuse this as one of our defenses, where the distance from each edge of the image is cropped by U[10, 50], independently for each edge.
comments.explanation: The authors do give useful parameter ranges for the random preprocessing operations, which is helpful. But for reproducibility, several key implementation details remain implicit. For Random Zoom, after independently cropping 10–50 pixels from each edge, the image presumably must be resized or padded back to the classifier’s input size, but the interpolation method, aspect-ratio handling, and boundary behavior are not stated. Similar ambiguities appear for the stochastic transforms: whether U[10,50] is continuous or integer-valued for pixel crop sizes, how many random draws are used per input, and whether the detector averages or samples once. These details can materially affect both benign accuracy and adversarial detection rates, especially on ImageNet.

comments.title: Defensive distillation discussion is incomplete and overstates the role of a perfect boundary
comments.quote: _**defensive distillation:**_ whether it can build its optimized network is critical to the effectiveness of this defense method. This is determined by whether the optimized network can accurately obtain the optimal discrimination boundary. If the learning effect of the boundary is perfect, the performance of defense against known attacks will be greatly improved. But for unknown attacks, the sample
comments.explanation: I can infer the authors’ intended point: distillation depends on the surrogate/distilled model’s learned decision boundary and may not handle unknown attacks well. That is broadly aligned with the preceding related-work discussion. However, the paragraph stops mid-thought at “But for unknown attacks, the sample,” so the actual caveat is missing. More substantively, the claim that a perfectly learned discrimination boundary would greatly improve defense against known attacks is not well justified. Defensive distillation was shown to produce gradient masking effects rather than robust boundaries in many settings, and a clean-data decision boundary can be accurate yet still have adversarially vulnerable regions. The passage needs to clarify what “optimal discrimination boundary” means and why it would imply adversarial robustness.

comments.title: Formal attack-success condition only covers untargeted attacks
comments.quote: given a normal example whose logit is L, the goal of the attacker is to perturb the example to get a new logit L2 such that rank(L,1) _̸_ = rank(L2,1)
comments.explanation: The condition is a standard way to express untargeted misclassification: the top-ranked class after perturbation differs from the original top-ranked class. That is fine if the authors are explicitly defining only untargeted attacks on initially correctly classified inputs. The surrounding paper, however, discusses attacks such as CW and JSMA, which are often evaluated in targeted as well as untargeted forms. For targeted attacks, the relevant condition is usually that the target class becomes top-ranked, not merely that the original top class changes. The notation also assumes that the original top-ranked logit corresponds to the true label, which should be stated if detection performance is measured only on correctly classified clean samples.

comments.title: Softmax probability is treated as a manifold-distance measure without justification
comments.quote: The size of the probability output represents the shape of the input x in the low-dimensional manifold mapping and the distance from this type of feature in the low-dimensional manifold mapping.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intended bridge: the classifier's final softmax output is being used as an observable proxy for how close an input is to learned class features or to a decision boundary, which is a plausible heuristic for a detector. However, the statement is stronger than what standard neural-network theory supports. Softmax probabilities are normalized exponentials of logits; they can reflect relative logit margins, but they are not calibrated distances to a data manifold or even reliable distances to a decision boundary unless additional assumptions or calibration arguments are supplied. In adversarial-example literature, deep nets are well known to assign high-confidence softmax outputs to off-manifold or nonsensical inputs, so this identification is especially delicate here. The surrounding context says the method uses probability divergence/manifold distance detection, but it does not provide a derivation or empirical calibration showing that softmax magnitude corresponds monotonically to manifold distance. Since this proxy is central to MDJ, the claim needs either a weaker wording or a concrete definition/validation of the distance being estimated.

comments.title: The core “simple method” for manifold-distance detection is missing or underspecified
comments.quote:
We use a simple method,

to reflect the transformation of the distance of the picture manifold.
comments.explanation: At first I suspected this might be an OCR omission, because the comma and blank line strongly suggest that an equation or algorithmic expression was present in the PDF. Looking at the surrounding context, the authors have introduced softmax probabilities, logits, and ranking, and the later tables include a baseline apparently called the manifold or “Math” method. However, even assuming an OCR loss, the passage as available does not define the actual statistic: whether it uses the maximum softmax probability, the original-class probability, top-1/top-2 margin, KL divergence before and after preprocessing, a rank change, or some other thresholded quantity. This is not a cosmetic omission because the experimental tables and Comb1–Comb3 procedures depend directly on this detector. A reader cannot reproduce the reported detection rates without the missing formula, threshold rule, and decision direction.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of “a single probability” leaves the detector criterion unclear
comments.quote:
Using only a single probability means that the effect is not significant for adversarial attacks, especially for adversarial samples that

have greatly crossed the boundary, their spatial mapping is almost the same as that of legitimate samples.
comments.explanation: The authors seem to be explaining why a detector based only on one softmax confidence value can fail: once an adversarial example is confidently classified as another class, its output may resemble a legitimate example from that class. That intuition is reasonable. What remains unclear is what “a single probability” refers to. It could mean the maximum softmax probability, the probability of the predicted class, the probability of the original/true class, or one class-specific coordinate. These choices behave very differently for targeted and untargeted attacks. The explanation also says the adversarial sample has “greatly crossed the boundary” but has spatial mapping “almost the same as legitimate samples,” which likely means legitimate samples of the adversarial target class, not legitimate samples of the original class. Without specifying this, a careful reader could misunderstand both the failure mode and the detection statistic.

comments.title: Detection threshold is repeatedly invoked but not defined
comments.quote: It can be obtained from TABLE 1 that the mainfold detection method performs better under black-box and gray-box attacks, and the ability to reach a detection model above 0.65 thresholds is weak.
comments.explanation: The tables report numbers that appear to be detection rates, and the text later discusses filtering samples above or below a threshold. I tried to resolve this from context by assuming 0.65 might be a chosen softmax-confidence threshold or a detection-rate benchmark. But the passage never defines what threshold is being applied, how it was selected, whether it is attack-specific or fixed globally, whether it was tuned on a validation set, or what false-positive rate on benign samples it induces. For an adversarial detector, threshold choice is a central experimental parameter; a high recall rate can be obtained trivially if the threshold is set to flag many benign inputs. The statement about “above 0.65 thresholds” is therefore not reproducible and makes the reported comparisons difficult to interpret.

comments.title: CW attacks are incorrectly characterized as changing only a limited number of pixels
comments.quote: Second, in a CW attack, the attack only changes a limited number of pixels. Moreover, in CW attack, it only changes a limited number of pixels, so the Next class as the targeted attack has a greater impact on the detection model, resulting in an increase in the detection rate of the least-likely class as the targeted attack.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to explain the behavior of the detector on Carlini-Wagner attacks, and the table indeed separates CWL0, CWL2, and CWL_i_, so they are aware of multiple CW norms. The problem is that the statement “CW attack only changes a limited number of pixels” is only appropriate, at most, for an L0-constrained CW attack. CW L2 and L∞ attacks generally optimize the perturbation magnitude under different norms and may alter many or all pixels by small amounts. Since the passage uses this pixel-sparsity explanation for CW attacks generally, including CWL2 and CWL_i_ rows, the technical explanation does not match the attack definitions used in the experiment.

comments.title: Explanation of CW least-likely vs next-class results is inconsistent with the table
comments.quote: the Next class as the targeted attack has a greater impact on the detection model, resulting in an increase in the detection rate of the least-likely class as the targeted attack.
comments.explanation: I read this as an attempt to interpret the LL and Next rows in Table 2. However, the prose is internally confusing: it says the Next-class targeted attack has a greater impact, but then says this results in an increase in the detection rate of the least-likely targeted attack. The table's “Math” column appears to show LL detection rates higher than Next for CWL0, CWL2, and CWL_i_ respectively: 0.97 vs 0.35, 0.94 vs 0.76, and 0.78 vs 0.24. Thus, if the intended point is that least-likely targets are easier to detect, the mention of Next having the greater impact is misleading; if the intended point is the opposite, the table does not support it. This is a localized but real inconsistency in the empirical interpretation.

comments.title: Comb1–Comb3 orchestration procedures are not specified enough to reproduce
comments.quote: _**Comb1**_ Comb1 is only to set up the threshold after performing the same image input transformation listed on the top of the table and use manifold detection method to make judgments. _**Comb2**_ Comb2 combines two single defense methods simply. In Comb2, we first performed image transformation on the samples and set up the threshold of manifold detection. After completing the image transformer, we checked the labels of the images to find out the examples which labels have changed before and after the transformation, as well as the examples whose output of softmax is higher than the threshold for filtering, to achieve the effect of classifying adversarial samples. _**Comb3**_ Comb3 firstly sets up a threshold, and perform manifold detection method on input samples. We filter samples above the threshold, perform image transformation on samples below the threshold, judge the change of the sample label, and use manifold detection method to detect again.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is understandable: combine preprocessing, label-change checks, and a softmax/manifold-distance threshold in different orders. But the operational details are underspecified in ways that affect the reported detection rates. It is unclear whether label change and threshold exceedance are combined with OR or AND logic, what “above the threshold” means semantically, whether the threshold is on maximum probability, probability drop, divergence, or margin, how randomized preprocessing is sampled or repeated, whether true labels or predicted labels before preprocessing are used, and how thresholds are tuned. In Comb3, “filter samples above the threshold” could mean declaring them adversarial or declaring them benign, and the text does not make the decision flow precise. Because Tables 3 and 4 are the main evidence for the orchestration strategy, this lack of algorithmic specificity undermines reproducibility and the strength of the comparison.

comments.title: Reported detection rates are interpreted without corresponding benign false-positive rates
comments.quote: Table 1 and Table 2 shows the detection rates for successful adversarial examples for each attack method.
comments.explanation: It is useful that the authors evaluate detection on successful adversarial examples, because those are the attacks that matter most for a detector. However, a detector's usefulness cannot be assessed from adversarial detection rate alone. The same thresholds used to produce these recall rates may also reject many benign ImageNet inputs, especially because the proposed criterion is based on softmax confidence and preprocessing-induced label changes. The surrounding text discusses detection rates and thresholding but does not state the benign false-positive rate, ROC/AUC, precision, or accuracy on clean samples for these tables. Unless this is reported elsewhere, the reader cannot tell whether high values such as 0.95 or 1.00 reflect a discriminative detector or an overly aggressive threshold.

comments.title: Comb1 is called a random combination method without clear basis
comments.quote: For these three orchestration strategies, Comb1 is equivalent to a random combination method.
comments.explanation: I can see one possible interpretation: the preprocessing transformations themselves include randomized parameters, so applying one transformation followed by thresholding has some randomness. But Comb1, as just defined, is not obviously a “random combination method”; it applies the image transformation listed for that table row and then the manifold detector. It does not randomly combine multiple defenses, and it is not equivalent to the random weak-defense ensembles discussed earlier unless an additional random-selection procedure is being assumed. This matters because the authors use Comb1 as a baseline for arguing that orchestration is better than random combinations. The comparison would be clearer if Comb1 were formally defined as a random baseline or described simply as preprocessing plus manifold detection.

comments.title: Detection-rate improvements are interpreted as improved model accuracy/classification efficiency
comments.quote: It further confirms our conclusion that the Manifold Distance Judge method designed by applying orchestration can effectively improve the accuracy of the model.
comments.explanation: I first read this as a natural summary of the preceding detection-rate results: Comb3 appears to reject more adversarial inputs than Comb1/Comb2. However, in the surrounding context MDJ is explicitly presented as an external detector that does not modify the protected classifier, and the reported quantities in Tables 3–5 are detection rates/recall, not clean classification accuracy or classifier accuracy after defense. A detector can improve system-level robustness or adversarial rejection while leaving the underlying model accuracy unchanged, and it can also harm effective accuracy if false positives on benign samples are high. The same concern reappears in the conclusion where the paper says the tables evaluate the 'classification efficiency' of the defense model. Unless the paper separately reports accepted-sample classification accuracy and false-positive rates on benign data, this wording overstates what the experiments establish.

comments.title: Table 5 comparison appears to use recall alone, without controlling false positives or thresholds
comments.quote: We compare our results with Feature Squeezing(Xu et al., 2017) in Table 5. Comb represent the Com3 in the previous Section4. We configured the Feature Squeezing detectors on ImageNet following the same experiment set-up d in Feature Squeezing paper and reported the detection performance with our target models and the detection dataset. It shows that our recall value on the same white box attack is significantly higher than feature squeezing, especially on CW attacks, we can detect all pairs of samples, and the best classification rate of feature squeezing is only 0.87.
comments.explanation: The authors are right to compare against Feature Squeezing, since it is a closely related preprocessing/detection baseline. What remains unclear is whether the comparison is made at a matched operating point. Adversarial detection performance depends strongly on the threshold: a detector can obtain very high recall simply by rejecting many benign inputs. Feature Squeezing conventionally reports or tunes detection thresholds with respect to benign false-positive rates, and a fair detector comparison usually needs ROC/AUC, recall at a fixed FPR, or at least the same validation protocol for threshold selection. Here Table 5 reports only recall, and the prose does not specify the benign false-positive rate, thresholds, squeezers used, or calibration procedure for either Feature Squeezing or MDJ. Therefore the conclusion that MDJ is 'significantly higher' than Feature Squeezing is not fully supported by the reported metric alone.

comments.title: Prose claim that all CW samples are detected conflicts with the reported Table 5 values
comments.quote: especially on CW attacks, we can detect all pairs of samples, and the best classification rate of feature squeezing is only 0.87.
comments.explanation: I checked whether this might be a loose paraphrase of the CW rows in Table 5. Several CW entries for Comb are indeed 1.00, so the authors' intended point—that MDJ performs very well on CW attacks—is supported. However, the table also lists the CWL_i_ / Next row with Comb recall 0.99, not 1.00. Assuming CWL_i_ is an OCR rendering of CWL∞, this is still a CW attack row. Thus the statement that the method can 'detect all pairs of samples' is inconsistent with the table as printed. The same sentence also calls Feature Squeezing's 0.87 a 'classification rate', while the table labels the metric as Recall. That metric mismatch could mislead readers about what is being compared.

comments.title: The Table 5 'Comb' configuration is underspecified despite multiple Comb3 variants
comments.quote: Comb represent the Com3 in the previous Section4.
comments.explanation: It is plausible that 'Com3' is an OCR or typographical variant of 'Comb3', and the table column 'Comb' is intended to denote the authors' strongest orchestration strategy. But earlier sections describe Comb3 as a strategy that can be paired with different preprocessing transformations and with different manifold/probability threshold choices. The passage itself mentions Random Zoom and Median Filter as different successful preprocessing choices, while the conclusion later says the optimized input transformation chose Median Filter and the math method selected the difference method. Table 5 does not specify which preprocessing method, threshold, probability-divergence statistic, randomization policy, or aggregation over random transformations was used. Because these choices can materially affect recall and false-positive behavior, a reader could not reproduce the comparison from the information given.

comments.title: Claims of significant improvement and coverage of 'all attacks' are stronger than the evidence described
comments.quote: We can see that for each preprocessing method, the detection rates of Comb1, Comb2, and Comb3 are rising significantly. Meanwhile, in a white-box attack scenario, a reasonably orchestrated Comb3 defense strategy, such as the Random Zoom method used in the preprocessing stage, can achieve a detection rate of more than 95% for all attacks. In the black-box attack scenario, the Comb3 combined defense strategy uses methods, such as Random Zoom and Median Filter in the preprocessing stage, to achieve a detection rate of 85% for all attacks.
comments.explanation: The intended conclusion is understandable: the orchestration variants apparently improve detection rates across the evaluated attacks, and Comb3 performs best. However, the wording introduces two possible overclaims. First, 'rising significantly' sounds like a statistical claim, but no confidence intervals, repeated-run variance, or significance test are described; this is especially relevant because several preprocessing methods are randomized. Second, 'for all attacks' could be read as a broad robustness statement, while the evidence appears limited to the finite attack suite and parameter settings tested in the paper. A more precise claim would be 'for all evaluated attacks under these settings' and, if statistical significance is intended, would require variance estimates or hypothesis tests.

comments.title: Citation appears to support poisoning attacks rather than the evasion attacks discussed in context
comments.quote: Luis Mu˜noz-Gonz´alez, Battista Biggio, Ambra Demontis, Andrea Paudice, Vasin Wongrassamee, Emil C Lupu, and Fabio Roli. Towards poisoning of deep learning algorithms with back-gradient optimization. In _Proceedings of the 10th ACM Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Security_ , pp. 27–38, 2017.
comments.explanation: The reference itself appears bibliographically plausible, and the cited work is indeed about using gradient-based/back-gradient optimization in an adversarial machine-learning setting. However, checking the surrounding context, this citation is used in the paper’s description of a “gradient optimization attack model” where an attacker knows the target model and generates adversarial samples such as FGSM, BIM, DeepFool, JSMA, and CW. Those are primarily test-time evasion/adversarial-example attacks. By contrast, the cited Muñoz-González et al. paper is about poisoning deep learning algorithms via back-gradient optimization, i.e., a training-time data-poisoning setting. A reader could therefore reasonably infer that this reference supports the white-box evasion-attack formulation, when it is actually about a different threat model. This is fixable by citing standard evasion-attack references for the general claim, or by clarifying that the citation is only meant to illustrate gradient-based adversarial optimization more broadly.
